income_tax.statute: HB 463 (2026), signed May 11, 2026, retroactive to January 1, 2026: flat individual income tax rate of 4.99% for tax year 2026. The bill's larger standard deduction ($15,000 single / $30,000 joint) begins tax year 2027; for 2026 the deduction remains $12,000 single / $24,000 married filing jointly.
omissions: 2026-2028 exclusions for tips and overtime pay ($1,750 each)
non-wage income (interest, dividends, capital gains, business income)
income_tax.note: The receipt models a wage salary against the flat rate and standard deduction only. It is an apportionment illustration, not tax advice or a filing estimate.

consumption_model.source: US Bureau of Labor Statistics, Consumer Expenditure Surveys, 2024, quintiles of income before taxes (interview + diary integrated tables).
taxable_nonfood_categories: entertainment commodities (pets, toys, hobbies, audio/visual equipment)
excluded_categories: vehicle purchases (Georgia levies TAVT instead of sales tax)
motor fuel (excise-taxed, exempt from most local rates)
caveats: Shares are hand-derived from the CES 2024 quintile tables: the taxable-nonfood categories above summed per quintile, divided by that quintile's mean pre-tax income. They are national consumer-unit averages, not Georgia-specific.
A CES consumer unit is a household, not an individual filer; a single salary entered here stands in for household income.
Groceries (food at home) are exempt from Georgia's 4% state sales tax but pay the local cents; the calculator applies only the local rate to the food-at-home base.
Between the quintile mean incomes the shares are interpolated linearly; below the first and above the fifth they are clamped.
